sections.title: Case Study: LiteLLM Supply Chain Attack (March 24, 2026)
items: Attack vector: Compromised Trivy executable distributed via PyPI (renovate bug), affecting litellm >=1.11.0
Impact: All API keys and server environment variables stolen — LLM provider keys, vector DB credentials, Langfuse credentials, RAG data
Scale: ~5,000-10,000 affected organizations (major banks, Fortune 500, AI safety orgs)
Financial services exposure: Banking AI assistants, customer-facing LLM apps, internal coding assistants, RAG systems with proprietary data
Root cause: Standard software supply chain compromise invisible to AI audit ecosystem — AI-specific governance gap

sections.title: Key Hypotheses Formed
items: H1 (Accountability Horizon): AI supply chain liability is structurally irreducible — institutions bear risk for harms beyond their visibility, and due diligence cannot close the horizon

H2 (Modularity Trap): The engineering practices that make AI development scalable are the same structures that dislocate accountability — individual behavior change cannot fix structural accountability failures
H3 (Audit Gap): AI audit ecosystems evaluate model behavior, not software supply chain security — structural reform of audit scope is required to address the LiteLLM problem class

sections.title: SOWHAT Updates
items: ta22-ta24: AI BOM requirements, audit gap, LiteLLM response (trust-professionals)
eb20-eb21: Software tooling governance, accountability horizon (executives)
ar21-ar22: MAS/APRA framework gaps, mandatory AI BOM requirements (regulators)

entries.title: Session 14: First Principles of Trust — Verification Collapse, Role-Sensitive Oversight, and Borderless Liability
entries.summary: Ran a first-principles deep-research session focused on the provenance of trust, meaningful human oversight at agentic scale, and liability when per-action human review is infeasible. Discovery covered 18 searches across web and arXiv queries. Deep-read 8 sources. Core findings: (1) GenAI erodes the background verification environment, not just output quality; (2) oversight must be designed around verifyability and agent role, not generic HITL placement; (3) better traces can raise reviewer confidence without improving accuracy; (4) agentic AI business cases systematically underprice verification burden; (5) transboundary AI harms pressure liability regimes toward compensation readiness and away from localised reviewer blame.
sourcesRead: Zhu (2025) CSIRO Designing Meaningful Human Oversight

sections.title: Papers Analyzed
items: p147 Ferrara (2026) — synthetic reality and verification collapse
p148 Shi & DiFranzo (2026) — role-sensitive oversight divergence
p149 Grunde-McLaughlin et al. (2026) — traces, confidence, and verification limits
p150 Lobentanzer (2026) — expectation-realisation gap for agentic AI
p151 Enyedi (2026) — human-certified module repositories
p152 Tran (2026) — borderless AI liability architecture
p153 Lange (2025) — epistemic deference to AI
p154 Zhu (2025) — solve–verify asymmetry and evaluative agency

sections.title: New Hypotheses Strengthened
items: H4 (Verification Collapse): AI trust fails when the institutional ability to verify claims is degraded, even before any specific model output is shown false
H5 (Verifyability Principle): meaningful human oversight only exists where humans retain independent evaluative agency under realistic time and evidence constraints

H6 (Compensation Shift): when AI harms are transboundary and action volume outruns review capacity, liability frameworks must shift from reviewer blame toward design accountability and compensation readiness

sections.title: SOWHAT Updates
items: ta25: trust-claim register with epistemic status and decay tests
ex22: price agentic AI on net value after verification burden
r23: mandate epistemic-status disclosure for AI trust claims

entries.title: First Principles of Trust — Provenance, HITL at Scale, Liability Restructuring
entries.summary: This session focused on the first principles of AI trust, responding to supervisor feedback. Three major research questions were investigated: (1) provenance of trust — what is ground truth vs assumptions vs proxies, and which trust properties are certified once vs continuously monitored; (2) what 'meaningful human in the loop' actually means at scale for agentic AI; (3) how liability and accountability frameworks should be restructured when human oversight is infeasible. A success case study (Singapore MAS thematic review) was examined.

sections.title: Papers Added (10)
items: p093: Ferrario & Facchini (2026) — Epistemology of Human-AI Complementarity. Core philosophical paper on why complementarity is ex-post (knowable only after ground truth) and how trust properties decompose by temporal degradation profile.
p094: Quattrociocchi, Capraro & Perc (2026) — Seven Epistemological Fault Lines. Foundational taxonomy of where human and AI cognition structurally diverge; introduces Epistemia condition.
p095: Lukas et al. (2026) — Epistemic Compression and Fidelity Paradox. Why training accuracy is an anti-proxy for robustness in Shifting Regime domains; Epistemic Compression framework.
p096: Harvard JOLT — Redefining Human Oversight for AI Negligence. Courts shifting from individual action review to systemic governance adequacy standard.
p097: Masood et al. — Operationalizing HITL at Enterprise Scale. Aviation CRM analogy, simulator training, automation complacency, five operational practices.
p098: Thomson Geer (Hayden Delaney) — Agentic AI Liability (Australian Law). Apparent authority, Air Canada chatbot precedent, directors' duties.
p099: Global Legal Insights — Cross-Jurisdictional AI Liability Analysis. Responsibility gaps, nuclear liability model, EU product liability directive update.
p100: MAS — AI Model Risk Management (Singapore). Success case study: thematic review methodology, published guidance from observed field practice.
p101: Durán & Pozzi (2025) — Trust and Trustworthiness in AI (Philosophy & Technology). First philosophical survey of trust/trustworthiness/reliance in AI.
p102: Strata Identity — Human-in-the-Loop: A 2026 Guide. HITL/HOTL/HOT three-tier framework, time-boxed decision lanes, challenge-and-response protocols.

sections.title: Themes Identified (5)
items: t061: Trust Property Decomposition — Temporal Degradation Profiles Are First-Principles, Not Framework Detail (very high confidence)
t062: Reliance Is a Risk-Management Proxy, Not Trust: Why This Distinction Changes Everything (very high confidence)
t063: Meaningful Human Oversight = Operational Skill, Not Documentation — The Aviation Inflection Point (very high confidence)
t064: Agentic AI Liability — Diffused Accountability Creates Structural Gaps, and Courts Are Starting to Close Them (very high confidence)
t065: Singapore MAS Thematic Review Model — Regulatory Empiricism Beats Framework Theorizing (high confidence)

sections.title: Original Hypotheses (3)
items: H1 (Trust Property Temporal Decomposition Theorem): Trustworthiness decomposes into design-time-certifiable, continuously-monitored, and change-triggered properties. In Shifting Regime domains (finance, medicine), even design-time properties require post-deployment monitoring because the deployment environment changes the effective properties of the system. Governance must be multi-modal.

H2 (Proxyification of Trust): In AI contexts, reliance on proxies (model cards, SOC 2 audits, benchmark scores) is risk management of epistemic uncertainty, not trust in the philosophical sense. Organisations that conflate the two are managing the appearance of trust, not trust itself.

H3 (HITL Legitimacy Condition): Human oversight only constitutes a meaningful governance control when: (a) the human has evaluative agency — ability to understand, judge, verify, redirect, or substitute; (b) has practiced under realistic pressure; (c) is matched to solve-verify asymmetry. Where verification is infeasible, oversight must be replaced by structural constraints, abstention criteria, or redesign.

sections.title: Key Findings
items: Supervisor Q1 (provenance): Trust properties decompose into temporal categories. Design-time certifiable (interpretability architecture, fairness constraints), continuously-monitored (calibration drift, alignment stability), change-triggered (competence on new contexts). Ground truth is non-stationary in financial services (Shifting Regime), making training accuracy unreliable as a trust signal.

Supervisor Q2 (HITL at scale): Cannot mean reviewing every action. Must mean risk-tiered oversight modes (HITL/HOTL/HOT by decision type) + trained evaluative agency + concentration of review where verification is feasible. Aviation CRM model is the right analogy.

Supervisor Q3 (liability): Courts shifting from individual action review to 'reasonable AI system' standard. Liability migrating from individual negligent acts to systemic governance inadequacy. Nuclear liability model (channel to operator + mandatory insurance) is the leading proposal.

Supervisor Q4 (success cases): Singapore MAS thematic review is the most concrete success case. Conducted structured review of actual AI MRM practices, documented observed good practice, published as industry guidance. This is regulatory empiricism vs. framework theorizing.

entries.summary: This session focused on Ray's four first-principles questions about AI trust: (1) provenance of trust — ground truth vs proxies; (2) meaningful human in the loop at scale; (3) liability/accountability restructuring; (4) success case studies. Key findings: Ground truth is always constructed (Jaton), making AI trust epistemic foundation socially negotiated; Carroll's Baier-based argument that reliability is the correct aspiration not trustworthiness; the automation paradox makes naive HITL infeasible; Singapore MindForge is the clearest success case; trust properties are temporally degraded requiring continuous monitoring not point-in-time certification.

sections.title: Provenance of Trust
items: Ground truth is always constructed by human annotators with cultural assumptions — not raw data
Trust in AI rests on governance of the data construction process, not objective ground truth
Carroll (Baier-based): AI should be reliable not trustworthy — trust creates obligations AI cannot bear
Trust properties degrade over time — accuracy, calibration, fairness require continuous monitoring

sections.title: Meaningful Human in the Loop
items: Bainbridge automation paradox: humans monitoring AI get worse at it over time
Naive per-action review is mathematically impossible at agentic AI scale
Meaningful Human Control = architectural provisions: understand, intervene, trace responsibility
Human oversight must be designed into system architecture, not added as personnel

sections.title: Liability & Accountability
items: Marketing AI as trustworthy creates trust obligations the AI cannot discharge
Blanco: trust relationships exist in human-AI interaction regardless of philosophical dispute
Organisations bear trust obligations whether or not formally recognised
Fiduciary governance duty for board-level AI oversight becoming mandatory

sections.title: Success Case Studies
items: Singapore MAS MindForge: 24 financial institutions co-developing AI risk management toolkit
First concrete, implementable financial services AI governance framework
Consortium model distributes innovation costs, creates shared ownership, produces practical outputs
DBS Bank: 2,000+ models deployed, projected economic impact SGD 1 billion+ — governance at scale

sections.title: Emerging Properties of Trust
items: AI as epistemic agent: actively shapes what humans know and believe — new governance category
Cognitive deskilling and epistemic drift when AI becomes primary knowledge intermediary
Knowledge sanctuaries: spaces preserving human epistemic independence
Model cards and data lineage become mandatory audit artefacts under EU AI Act from August 2026

entries.session: Trust Provenance, Meaningful Oversight, and Adaptive Liability
entries.summary: This session deepened Ray’s first-principles trust agenda by focusing on evidence-bearing provenance, meaningful oversight in agentic systems, liability restructuring under adaptive AI, and banking case studies that appear to work in practice. Main findings: provenance is shifting from narrative assurance to cryptographic evidence chains, but those chains still depend on an explicitly declared first trusted boundary; meaningful HITL at scale is a runtime control architecture problem, not a reviewer headcount problem; adaptive and continually changing systems weaken static certification and push liability toward actors who control post-deployment change; DBS provides a useful financial-services success case because governance is fused with operating model redesign, workforce training, and value measurement rather than treated as a compliance overlay.

sections.title: Trust Provenance and Evidence Chains
items: IETF lifecycle attestation draft binds training data, model weights, deployment state, and inference outputs into one cryptographic chain
VAP framework extends provenance into evidence-pack design with completeness invariants and override coverage metrics
Chain of epistemic custody sharpens the distinction between source truth, transit integrity, and reasoning-time confabulation
Original hypothesis: the key governance question is not whether provenance exists, but where the first trusted boundary begins

sections.title: Meaningful Human Oversight at Scale
items: Harvard JOLT shows naive HITL can be legally incoherent when humans cannot realistically verify model reasoning
Strata operational analysis reinforces that oversight must be dynamically risk-tiered and identity-enforced
Meaningful oversight is best understood as runtime intervention capacity with real stop-power, context, and bounded time windows
Original hypothesis: human oversight quality should be regulated as an operational resilience property, not a governance disclosure item

sections.title: Adaptive Liability and Moving Regulatory Targets
items: Lawfare argues continual learning destabilises regulation built around fixed model identities and fixed capability snapshots
White House framework leans toward lighter upstream developer liability and national uniformity, showing policy divergence is widening
AI LEAD Act proposal illustrates layered developer/deployer liability as a more explicit statutory allocation model
Synthesis: the most coherent future rule is liability by control over change, not by original authorship alone

sections.title: Financial Services Success Case
items: DBS PURE framework ties ethics to operational decisions: Purposeful, Unsurprising, Respectful, Explainable
DBS governance is reinforced by data foundations, model governance, staged rollout, cross-functional review, and internal training
Computer Weekly reporting suggests DBS is redesigning workflows, structures, and value capture around AI rather than layering governance on top
MindForge plus DBS suggests successful banking governance is an operating model redesign, not a policy artifact

sections.title: Self-Critique
items: Several sources in this session are draft standards, practitioner essays, or institutional self-descriptions rather than peer-reviewed studies
That weakens causal confidence, but the convergence across technical, legal, and operational sources is still analytically useful

A philosophy PhD critique would likely press harder on the distinction between evidence integrity and truth itself, and on whether provenance can ever resolve normative disputes embedded in labels and targets

The strongest surviving claim is modest: AI trust becomes more defensible when institutions separate observed facts, inferred judgments, and authorized actions, then make each layer independently inspectable
